title: TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods
abstract: We propose TruthfulQA, a benchmark to measure whether a language model is truthful in generating answers to questions. The benchmark comprises 817 questions that span 38 categories, including health, law, finance and politics. We tested GPT-3, GPT-Neo/GPT-J, GPT-2 and a T5-based model. The best model was truthful on 58% of questions, while human performance was 94%. Larger models were generally less truthful, a result that is the inverse of the scaling found in other NLU tasks. The models generated many false answers that mimic popular misconceptions and have the potential to deceive humans.
domain: general (38 categories: health, law, finance, politics, etc.)
hallucination_rate: Best model 58% truthful (42% hallucination); larger models less truthful; human baseline 94% truthful

title: FActScore: Fine-grained Atomic Evaluation of Factual Precision in Long Form Text Generation
abstract: We introduce FActScore (Factual precision in Atomicity Score), a new evaluation that breaks a generation into a series of atomic facts and computes the percentage of atomic facts supported by a reliable knowledge source. We evaluate people biographies generated by several LLMs. InstructGPT achieves a FActScore of 71.6, ChatGPT 71.5, and PerplexityAI 89.3. Retrieval augmentation and smaller more recent models show higher factual precision.
hallucination_rate: InstructGPT: 28.4% atomic facts unsupported; ChatGPT: 28.5% unsupported; PerplexityAI (retrieval-augmented): 10.7% unsupported

title: HaluEval: A Large-Scale Hallucination Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are prone to generate hallucinations, i.e., content that conflicts with the source or cannot be verified by factual knowledge. To understand what types of content and to what extent LLMs are apt to hallucinate, we introduce HaluEval, a large-scale collection of generated and human-annotated hallucinated samples for evaluating the performance of LLMs in recognizing hallucinations. HaluEval includes 5,000 general user queries with ChatGPT responses and 30,000 task-specific examples from three tasks (QA, knowledge-grounded dialogue, text summarization). ChatGPT recognizes only about 62% of hallucinated content in its own output.
hallucination_rate: ChatGPT fails to recognize ~38% of hallucinations in its own output; hallucination recognition accuracy ~62%

title: HalluLens: LLM Hallucination Benchmark
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often generate responses that deviate from user input or training data, a phenomenon known as hallucination. This paper introduces a comprehensive hallucination benchmark, incorporating both new extrinsic and existing intrinsic evaluation tasks, built upon clear taxonomy of hallucination. We disentangle LLM hallucination from factuality, proposing a clear taxonomy that distinguishes between extrinsic and intrinsic hallucinations. Our benchmark includes dynamic test set generation to mitigate data leakage and ensure robustness.
hallucination_rate: Provides comprehensive benchmark rates across models; dynamic test sets to prevent data leakage saturation

title: A Survey on Hallucination in Large Language Models: Principles, Taxonomy, Challenges, and Open Questions
abstract: The emergence of large language models (LLMs) has marked a significant breakthrough in natural language processing (NLP), fueling a paradigm shift in information acquisition. Nevertheless, LLMs are prone to hallucination, generating plausible yet nonfactual content. This survey begins with an innovative taxonomy of hallucination in the era of LLM and then delves into the factors contributing to hallucinations. Subsequently, we present a thorough overview of hallucination detection methods and benchmarks. Our discussion then transfers to representative methodologies for mitigating LLM hallucinations.
models_tested: Survey covering GPT-3/4, ChatGPT, LLaMA, PaLM, and others
hallucination_rate: Survey paper aggregating rates from multiple studies; documents hallucination across QA, summarization, dialogue tasks

title: WikiChat: Stopping the Hallucination of Large Language Model Chatbots by Few-Shot Grounding on Wikipedia
abstract: This paper presents WikiChat, the first few-shot LLM-based chatbot that almost never hallucinates and has high conversationality and low latency. WikiChat is grounded on the English Wikipedia. Using a novel hybrid human-and-LLM evaluation methodology, we show that our best system achieves 97.3% factual accuracy in simulated conversations. It significantly outperforms all retrieval-based and LLM-based baselines. WikiChat achieves 97.9% factual accuracy in conversations with human users about recent topics, 55.0% better than GPT-4.
hallucination_rate: GPT-4 baseline: ~42.9% factual accuracy on recent topics; WikiChat: 97.9% factual accuracy (2.1% hallucination rate)

title: Hallucination in Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Survey, Taxonomy, and Mitigation Strategies
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance in a wide range of natural language processing operations, but their propensity to produce plausible-sounding, but factually incorrect information is often known as hallucination. We evaluate five top LLMs -- GPT-4, Claude 3 Opus, Gemini Pro 1.5, Llama 3 70B, and Mistral 8x7B -- on the TruthfulQA, HaluEval, and FActScore benchmarks and find hallucination rates of 18.7% to 34.2%. The RLHF+RAG hybrid achieves the best accuracy of 91.5%.
hallucination_rate: 18.7%--34.2% across five LLMs; RLHF+RAG hybrid reduces to 8.5%

title: How Much Do LLMs Hallucinate across Languages? On Realistic Multilingual Estimation of LLM Hallucination
abstract: We aim to quantify the extent of LLM hallucination across languages in knowledge-intensive long-form question answering (LFQA). We train a multilingual hallucination detection model and conduct a large-scale study across 30 languages and 6 open-source LLM families. Our analysis shows that LLMs hallucinate more tokens in high-resource languages due to longer responses, but that actual hallucination rates (normalized for length) seem uncorrelated with language digital footprint size. Smaller LLMs hallucinate more, and LLMs with broader language support display higher hallucination rates.
hallucination_rate: Varies by model and language; smaller models hallucinate significantly more; broader language support correlates with higher hallucination rates

title: Self-Logical Consistency Assessment of Large Language Models for Patient Feedback Classification
abstract: This study evaluated the Self-Logical Consistency Assessment (SLCA), a method designed to enhance LLM feedback classification reliability by enforcing a logically-structured chain of thought. SLCA reduced logical hallucinations among detected categories from 15.80% to 0.51% with GPT-4 and from 7.17% to 1.67% with Llama-3.1, with residual errors confined to the emergency feedback category.
hallucination_rate: GPT-4 baseline: 15.80% logical hallucination, reduced to 0.51% with SLCA; Llama-3.1: 7.17% reduced to 1.67%

title: Exploring the Boundaries of Reality: Investigating the Phenomenon of Artificial Intelligence Hallucination in Scientific Writing Through ChatGPT References
abstract: This study investigates the phenomenon of AI hallucination in scientific writing by examining references generated by ChatGPT. The study found that ChatGPT fabricated a significant proportion of scientific references when asked to generate citations, highlighting critical concerns about using LLMs for academic writing without verification.
hallucination_rate: High rate of fabricated references in ChatGPT-generated citations; landmark early study on citation hallucination

title: AI in conjunctivitis research: assessing ChatGPT and DeepSeek for etiology, intervention, and citation integrity via hallucination rate analysis
abstract: In this study, ChatGPT and DeepSeek are utilized as AI models to assist in diagnosis based on the responses generated to clinical questions. DeepSeek achieved a better performance with a 7% hallucination rate compared to ChatGPT's 13%. Claude demonstrated perfect 100% accuracy in binary classification, significantly outperforming ChatGPT's 62.5% accuracy.
hallucination_rate: DeepSeek: 7%; ChatGPT: 13%; Claude: 0% (binary classification)

title: Importing Phantoms: Measuring LLM Package Hallucination Vulnerabilities
abstract: We analyze package hallucination behaviour in LLMs across popular programming languages examining both existing package references and fictional dependencies. We discover that package hallucination rate is predicated not only on model choice, but also programming language, model size, and specificity of the coding task request. The Pareto optimality boundary between code generation performance and package hallucination is sparsely populated. We find an inverse correlation between package hallucination rate and the HumanEval coding benchmark.
hallucination_rate: Varies by model, language, and task specificity; inverse correlation with HumanEval scores; demonstrates security implications of code hallucination

title: Trustworthy AI for Medicine: Continuous Hallucination Detection and Elimination with CHECK
abstract: We present CHECK, a continuous-learning framework that integrates structured clinical databases with a classifier grounded in information theory to detect both factual and reasoning-based hallucinations. Evaluated on 1500 questions from 100 pivotal clinical trials, CHECK reduced LLama3.3-70B-Instruct hallucination rates from 31% to 0.3%. Its classifier generalized across medical benchmarks, achieving AUCs of 0.95-0.96, including on the MedQA (USMLE) benchmark.
hallucination_rate: LLama3.3-70B baseline: 31% hallucination; reduced to 0.3% with CHECK framework; GPT-4o USMLE passing rate boosted by 5pp to 92.1%

title: Measuring and Reducing LLM Hallucination without Gold-Standard Answers via Expertise-Weighting
abstract: LLM hallucination is currently a major threat to the trustworthiness and reliability of LLMs. We propose Factualness Evaluations via Weighting LLMs (FEWL), an innovative hallucination metric designed for scenarios when gold-standard answers are absent. FEWL leverages answers from off-the-shelf LLMs as a proxy of gold-standard answers. Extensive experiment results on TruthfulQA, CHALE, and HaluEval datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of FEWL.
models_tested: Multiple LLMs as reference models (evaluated on TruthfulQA, CHALE, HaluEval)
hallucination_rate: Provides calibrated hallucination measurement without gold answers; validated across TruthfulQA, CHALE, HaluEval

title: The Law of Knowledge Overshadowing: Towards Understanding, Predicting, and Preventing LLM Hallucination
abstract: We propose a novel concept: knowledge overshadowing, where model's dominant knowledge can obscure less prominent knowledge during text generation. Central to our approach is the log-linear law, which predicts that the rate of factual hallucination increases linearly with the logarithmic scale of (1) Knowledge Popularity, (2) Knowledge Length, and (3) Model Size. Built on overshadowing effect, we propose CoDa decoding strategy, which notably enhances model factuality on Overshadow (27.9%), MemoTrap (13.1%) and NQ-Swap (18.3%).
hallucination_rate: Hallucination rate follows log-linear law with knowledge popularity, length, and model size; CoDa reduces hallucination by 13.1-27.9% on benchmarks

title: A framework to assess clinical safety and hallucination rates of LLMs for medical text summarisation
abstract: Integrating large language models (LLMs) into healthcare can enhance workflow efficiency and patient care by automating tasks such as summarising consultations. However, the fidelity between LLM outputs and ground truth information is vital to prevent miscommunication that could lead to compromise in patient safety. We propose a framework comprising (1) an error taxonomy for classifying LLM outputs, (2) an experimental structure for iterative comparisons in our LLM document generation pipeline, (3) a clinical safety framework to evaluate the harms of errors, and (4) a graphical user interface, CREOLA, to facilitate these processes. Our clinical error metrics were derived from 18 experimental configurations involving LLMs for clinical note generation, consisting of 12,999 clinician-annotated sentences. We observed a 1.47% hallucination rate and a 3.45% omission rate. By refining prompts and workflows, we successfully reduced major errors below previously reported human note-taking rates, highlighting the framework's potential for safer clinical documentation.
hallucination_rate: 1.47% hallucination rate, 3.45% omission rate

title: Accuracy, Consistency, and Hallucination of Large Language Models When Analyzing Unstructured Clinical Notes in Electronic Medical Records
abstract: Researchers have been exploring advanced artificial intelligence (AI) techniques such as large language models (LLMs) to enhance data extraction from clinical notes in electronic medical records (EMRs). This study investigates the utility and efficacy of ChatGPT-4 in extracting helmet status from clinical narratives of patients involved in micromobility-related accidents. Three different levels of prompting detail for the LLM (low, intermediate, and high) were employed, and agreement with text string-search approach was measured using Cohen kappa test statistics. The high-detail prompt showed strong agreement. Performance statistics were calculated for a criterion standard review in a random sample of 400 records.
hallucination_rate: Cohen kappa 0.74 (low-detail), 0.53 (intermediate-detail) agreement; hallucination rate varied by prompt detail level

title: Medical Hallucination in Foundation Models and Their Impact on Healthcare
abstract: Foundation Models that are capable of processing and generating multi-modal data have transformed AI's role in medicine. However, a key limitation of their reliability is hallucination. This paper examines the unique characteristics, causes, and implications of medical hallucinations. Contributions include (1) a taxonomy for understanding medical hallucinations, (2) benchmarking models using medical hallucination dataset and physician-annotated LLM responses to real medical cases, and (3) a multi-national clinician survey. Results reveal that Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Search Augmented Generation can effectively reduce hallucination rates. However, non-trivial levels of hallucination persist.
hallucination_rate: Non-trivial persistent rates; CoT and SAG reduce but do not eliminate hallucinations

title: Navigating artificial intelligence (AI) accuracy: A meta-analysis of hallucination incidence in large language model (LLM) responses to oncology questions
abstract: This meta-analysis examines the pooled incidence of hallucination in LLM responses to oncology questions. 39 studies involving 6,523 responses were included. The pooled hallucination incidence was 12.47% (95% CI: 8.53-17.82%). Patient-oriented prompts showed a hallucination rate of 14.34%, while physician-oriented prompts showed 10.53%. GPT-3.5 had a higher hallucination rate (15.35%) compared to GPT-4 (8.55%). Simple prompts had higher hallucination rates (14.83%) vs contextual prompts (9.16%).

title: MEDAI-LLM-SUMM: a reporting checklist for medical text summarization studies using large language models
abstract: A comprehensive review revealed that only 7% of studies conducted external validation and 3% performed patient safety assessments, with hallucination rates ranging from 1.47% to 61.6%. This paper develops MEDAI-LLM-SUMM, the first specialized reporting checklist for research on medical text summarization using LLMs. The final checklist comprises 24 items organized into six sections.
hallucination_rate: Range: 1.47% to 61.6% across reviewed studies

title: K-QA: A Real-World Medical Q&A Benchmark
abstract: We construct K-QA, a dataset containing 1,212 patient questions originating from real-world conversations on a clinical online platform. We employ physicians to answer and decompose responses into self-contained statements. We formulate NLI-based evaluation metrics: (1) comprehensiveness and (2) hallucination rate. Findings indicate that in-context learning improves comprehensiveness, and augmented retrieval is effective in reducing hallucinations.
hallucination_rate: Quantified via NLI-based hallucination rate metric on 1,212 real patient questions
note: arXiv preprint - DOI registered with DataCite, not CrossRef

title: WorldBench: Quantifying Geographic Disparities in LLM Factual Recall
abstract: We present WorldBench, a benchmark composed of per-country data from the World Bank. In experiments on 20+ LLMs including GPT-4, Gemini, Llama-2, and Vicuna, we find significant biases based on region and income level. Error rates are 1.5 times higher for countries from Sub-Saharan Africa compared to North American countries. WorldBench also enables automatic detection of citation hallucination.
hallucination_rate: Error rates 1.5x higher for Sub-Saharan Africa vs North America; geographic disparity across 20 LLMs

title: Place Matters: Comparing LLM Hallucination Rates for Place-Based Legal Queries
abstract: We propose a methodology to obtain place-to-place metrics for LLM legal knowledge based on comparative law functionalism. We construct factual scenarios from Reddit posts seeking legal advice for family, housing, employment, crime and traffic issues. LLM summaries of relevant law in Los Angeles, London and Sydney are manually evaluated for hallucinations. The rate of hallucination of legal information by leading closed-source LLMs is significantly associated with place. Strong negative correlation between hallucination rate and frequency of majority response when sampled multiple times.
hallucination_rate: Significantly associated with place (Los Angeles vs London vs Sydney); quantified per jurisdiction
note: arXiv preprint - DOI registered with DataCite, not CrossRef

title: Evaluating LLM-Generated Legal Explanations for Regulatory Compliance in Social Media Influencer Marketing
abstract: Using 1,143 Instagram posts, we compare gpt-5-nano and gemini-2.5-flash-lite under three prompting strategies with controlled levels of legal knowledge. Both models perform strongly in classification (F1 up to 0.93), though performance drops by over 10 points on ambiguous cases. Taxonomy of reasoning errors: citation omissions (28.57%), unclear references (20.71%), hidden ads exhibiting highest miscue rate (28.57%).
hallucination_rate: Citation omissions 28.57%, unclear references 20.71%, hidden ads miscue rate 28.57%

title: MEDX-FE: A Robust LLM-Based Framework for Medical Feature Extraction with Matching Gate Hallucination Filtering
abstract: We introduce MEDX-FE, a framework that leverages LLMs to extract clinical features from patient notes. Central to our framework is the Matching Gate, a three-stage validation module designed to mitigate LLM hallucinations. MEDX-FE achieves micro F1 score of 0.96 on USMLE Step 2 CS dataset and maintains 0.946 with only 10 examples per case.
hallucination_rate: Matching Gate reduces hallucinations; F1=0.96 (full training), 0.946 (10 examples)

title: A medically grounded LLM agent-based tool to detect patient safety events in medical records
abstract: We present SAFE-AI, a method for clinical decision making that combines clinical expert knowledge with LLMs in an ontology-driven model that minimizes hallucinations using strict rules. Tested on 18,402 lines from 300 EMS clinical charts. Achieved 97.9% accuracy in detecting epinephrine overdoses and 91.6% accuracy in identifying delays in administration, greatly outperforming baseline LLMs.
hallucination_rate: Baseline LLMs significantly underperformed; SAFE-AI achieved 97.9% accuracy (overdose detection), 91.6% (delay detection)

title: Trustworthy Medical Imaging with Large Language Models: A Study of Hallucinations Across Modalities
abstract: This study examines hallucinations in two directions: image to text (LLMs generate reports from X-ray, CT, MRI scans) and text to image (models create medical images from clinical prompts). We analyze errors such as factual inconsistencies and anatomical inaccuracies across imaging modalities.
hallucination_rate: Quantified factual inconsistencies and anatomical inaccuracies across X-ray, CT, MRI modalities

title: Clinical pathway-aware large language models for reliable and transparent medical dialogue
abstract: We proposed MedKP, which integrates external medical knowledge graph and encodes clinical pathways. Performance compared with GPT-4o and LLaMA3.1-70B. MedKP (6B) achieved best overall performance. For clinical relevance, macro F1 of 31.41 on MedDG (previous best 24.76, improved 30.41%) and 26.62 on KaMed (previous best 20.67, improved 25.62%). Hallucination and consistency evaluated via LLM-based judge and human evaluation.
hallucination_rate: MedKP reduced hallucination vs GPT-4o and LLaMA3.1-70B; F1 improved 25-30% over baselines

title: SelfCheckGPT: Zero-Resource Black-Box Hallucination Detection for Generative Large Language Models
abstract: SelfCheckGPT is a simple sampling-based approach to fact-check the output of black-box LLMs in a zero-resource fashion. The key idea is that if an LLM has knowledge of a given concept, sampled responses are likely to be similar and contain consistent facts. Inconsistencies across samples suggest hallucination. The method requires no external databases and works with any generative LLM.
hallucination_rate: Demonstrated effective detection via consistency-based scoring

title: A Survey of Multimodal Hallucination Evaluation and Detection
abstract: Comprehensive survey of hallucination evaluation benchmarks and detection methods for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) across Image-to-Text and Text-to-Image tasks, proposing taxonomy based on faithfulness and factuality.
hallucination_rate: Survey of reported rates across multimodal benchmarks
evaluation_method: Taxonomy of faithfulness and factuality evaluation methods

title: DefAn: Definitive Answer Dataset for LLM Hallucination Evaluation
abstract: This paper introduces a comprehensive benchmark dataset consisting of over 20,000 unique prompts (more than 75,000 prompts in total) across eight domains. In experiments, nine SoTA models were tested: GPT-4o, GPT-3.5, LLama 2 7B, LLama 3 8B, Gemini 1.0 Pro, Mixtral 8x7B, Zephyr 7B, Deepseek-r1-7b, and Qwen2.5-14B. Overall factual hallucination ranges from 48% to 82% on the public dataset and 31% to 76% on the hidden benchmark. Prompt Misalignment Hallucination ranges up to 95%. Average consistency ranges from 21% to 61%.
hallucination_rate: Factual hallucination: 48%–82% (public), 31%–76% (hidden); Prompt misalignment: up to 95%
evaluation_method: DefAn benchmark (20,000+ definitive-answer prompts across 8 domains)

title: Diagnosing Untruthfulness: A G-Eval and Bootstrap Analysis of LLM Failure Modes on TruthfulQA
abstract: This study moves beyond simple accuracy metrics to systematically diagnose the error patterns of GPT-3.5-turbo and GPT-4 on the challenging TruthfulQA benchmark. It created an 'error corpus' by collecting incorrect responses from both models. A more advanced model, gpt-4o, was employed as a judge within a G-Eval framework to classify each error into a fine-grained taxonomy. The analysis reveals that both models exhibit stable and dominant failure modes, with the repetition of common human misconceptions being the primary cause of untruthfulness.
hallucination_rate: Dominant failure mode: repetition of common human misconceptions
evaluation_method: G-Eval framework + bootstrap simulation on TruthfulQA

title: Academic Guidebook Chatbot: Performance Comparison of Fine-Tuned Mistral 7B and LlaMA-2 7B
abstract: This study develops a local document-based chatbot using open-source AI models (Mistral 7B and LLaMA-2 7B). Fine-tuning was performed using domain-specific datasets from Academic Guidebook. Fine-tuned Mistral 7B achieved the highest METEOR value of 0.40 and F1 of 0.78 (BERTScore). Fine-tuned Mistral 7B showed faster response time than LLaMA-2. Results showed that enhancements significantly improved performance, reduced hallucinations, and enhanced response quality.
hallucination_rate: Reduced via fine-tuning; METEOR 0.40, BERTScore F1 0.78 (best model)
evaluation_method: METEOR Score + BERTScore + response time measurement

title: LongHealth: A Question Answering Benchmark with Long Clinical Documents
abstract: We present the LongHealth benchmark, comprising 20 detailed fictional patient cases across various diseases, each containing 5090 to 6754 words. The benchmark challenges LLMs with 400 multiple-choice questions in three categories: information extraction, negation, and sorting. We evaluated eleven open-source LLMs with a minimum of 16,000 tokens. Highest accuracy was observed for Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct-2501 and Llama-4-Scout-17B-16E-Instruct. However, all models struggled significantly in tasks requiring identification of missing information.
hallucination_rate: All models struggled with missing information identification (clinical hallucination risk)

title: MEGA-RAG: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework with Multi-Evidence Guided Answer Refinement for Mitigating Hallucinations of LLMs in Public Health
abstract: We propose MEGA-RAG, integrating multi-source evidence retrieval (FAISS, BM25, and biomedical knowledge graphs), a cross-encoder reranker, and a discrepancy-aware refinement module. MEGA-RAG outperforms four baseline models (PubMedBERT, PubMedGPT, standalone LLM, and LLM with standard RAG), achieving a reduction in hallucination rates by over 40%. It also achieves the highest accuracy (0.7913), precision (0.7541), recall (0.8304), and F1 score (0.7904).
hallucination_rate: 40%+ reduction in hallucination rates vs baselines
evaluation_method: Multi-evidence RAG evaluation (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, hallucination rate)

title: Battling Misinformation: An Empirical Study on Adversarial Factuality in Open-Source Large Language Models
abstract: An empirical study examining adversarial factuality in open-source large language models. Evaluates how open-source LLMs handle adversarial misinformation prompts and measures their factuality under challenging conditions.
hallucination_rate: Adversarial factuality rates reported across open-source models
evaluation_method: Adversarial prompt evaluation for factuality in open-source LLMs

title: RAC: Efficient LLM Factuality Correction with Retrieval Augmentation
abstract: RAC proposes an efficient approach to correcting factual errors in LLM-generated text using retrieval augmentation. The method focuses on post-generation factuality correction rather than prevention, providing a complementary approach to RAG-based hallucination mitigation.
models_tested: Multiple LLMs evaluated for factuality correction
hallucination_rate: Post-generation correction effectiveness measured

title: Reducing LLM Hallucination Using Knowledge Distillation: A Case Study with Mistral Large and MMLU Benchmark
abstract: Explores using knowledge distillation to reduce hallucination in large language models. The approach transfers capabilities from a larger teacher model to a smaller student model using temperature scaling and intermediate layer matching. Testing via the MMLU benchmark demonstrated improved accuracy and reduced hallucination while remaining computationally efficient. Knowledge distillation is shown to be a practical pathway for deploying reliable language models requiring factual precision.
hallucination_rate: Improved accuracy and reduced hallucination via distillation (MMLU benchmark)
evaluation_method: MMLU benchmark evaluation + knowledge distillation comparison
